Did You Know? Windows' Shutdown Isn't Actually Shutting Down
Newer Windows versions added a "Fast Startup" feature. It genuinely improves boot times, but it bit me hard. Here's how to disable it.

Fast Startup is a feature added in newer Windows versions to improve user experience. When you click Shutdown, Windows saves the system state to a file, and on next boot it loads from that file — making the next boot faster.
It's similar to Hibernate, but Hibernate writes to disk while Fast Startup writes to a file.
Hibernate is safer because even if you lose power during hibernation, you're fine. Fast Startup is riskier because if the file gets corrupted, your next boot has issues.

Why am I bringing this up? My Windows 10 machine at home never had Fast Startup turned off. Over time, blue screens started getting more frequent. At first I thought it was Windows Update — tried uninstalling updates, no luck. Then I remembered Fast Startup. Disabled it, and the blue screens stopped.
It's pretty easy. Open Control Panel and find "System and Security".
In the popup, click "Choose what the power buttons do".
Choose what the power buttons do
You'll see "Turn on fast startup (recommended)" — it's greyed out. Click "Change settings that are currently unavailable" first.
Change settings that are currently unavailable
Uncheck "Turn on fast startup (recommended)" and click "Save changes".
From now on, clicking Shutdown actually shuts down — not the hibernation-like state.

Final note on why I started suspecting Fast Startup. My Windows 10 was fine at first, but every time an update ran, the next morning the machine would have issues. Checked update history, checked hardware — turns out my laptop battery was already dead, but updates themselves were fine.

Sometimes when you click "Update and shut down", Windows updates and then shuts down. As soon as the screen went dark, I'd reflexively unplug the power. But Windows might still have been writing files at that point — which could corrupt the file, leading to blue screens. After disabling Fast Startup, I watched things for a while and ran multiple updates (still on the dead battery), no blue screens at all. So I'm pretty sure Fast Startup was the issue.
After writing this post, I'm going to order a replacement laptop battery — going out without one is a hassle.
Newer Windows versions ship with 'Fast Startup' on by default — Shutdown actually dumps system state to a file and reloads it next boot. Why, and how to turn it off.
